coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based fluorescent probe for early diagnosis of pheochromocytoma through identification of circulating tumor cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Liping
Fang, Yiwei
Chen, Xinhe
Meng, Yang
Wang, Fei
Li, Caolong
coredata.dc:description: Pheochromocytoma (PCC), as a rare neuroendocrine tumor, is often missed or misdiagnosed because of its atypical clinical manifestations. To realize the early accurate diagnosis of PCC, we have selected circulating tumor cells (CTCs) with more complete biological information as biomarkers and developed a simple and novel fluorescence cytosensor. Octreotide-2,2′,2′′,2′’’- (1,4,7,10 -tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetrayl) tetraacetic acid (DOTA) modified magnetic Fe3O4 and signal amplification CDs@SiO2 nanospheres are prepared to capture and detect PCC-CTCs from peripheral blood via binding to the somatostatin receptor SSTR2 overexpressed on the surface of PCC cells. During the detection process, the target cells were separated and enriched by magnetic capture probes (Fe3O4-DOTA), and then signal probes (CDs@SiO2-DOTA) could also specifically bound to target cells to form the sandwich-like structure for fluorescence signal output. The proposed fluorescence cytosensor has revealed good sensitivity and selectivity for quantitative analysis of PCC-CTCs in the concentration of 5–1000 cells mL−1 with a LOD of 2 cells mL−1. More importantly, designed fluorescence cytosensor has shown good reliability and stability in complex serum samples. This strategy provides a new way for detection of PCC-CTCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pheochromocytoma
Early diagnosis
Circulating tumor cells
Fluorescence cytosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles/CDs@SiO2 to DOTA antibodies
Fluorescence analysis of PC12 cells by cytosensor
Fluorescence detection of PC12 cells in whole blood

National Key Research and Development Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program ( 2019YFC0312602 ) as well as Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region ( XZ202301YD0018C ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM images of CDs and particle size distribution). (b) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of CDs (Insert: The image of CDs under UV light). The XPS spectra of survey (c), C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), and O 1 s (f).

(a) The SEM images of CDs@SiO2. (b) XPS survey spectrum of CDs@SiO2. (c) Zeta-potential of Fe3O4-DOTA and CDs@SiO2-DOTA. (d) The FT-IR spectra of various species. (e) The FL excitation and emission profiles of CDs@SiO2-DOTA. (f) The FL profiles of CDs@SiO2-DOTA over excitation light range from 310 to 350 nm.

Micrographs of PC12 cells treated with (a) Fe3O4. (b) Fe3O4-DOTA (c) Fe3O4-DOTA + CDs@SiO2-DOTA. (d) Average fluorescence intensity of (b) and (c). (PC12 cells were stained by using DAPI and scale bars: 100 µm).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of various systems. The effects of (b) the ratio of VSP to VCP, (c) pH value, and (d) incubation time.

(a) Fluorescence signals of proposed cytosensor toward various contents of PC12 cells. (b) The linear fitting plots of fluorescence signals vs concentration of PC12 cells. (c) The fluorescence responses of various systems. (d) Fluorescence signals of this cytosensor toward various contents of PC12 cells in whole blood and PBS buffer.
Schematic illustration of capture, separation, and analysis of PCC-CTCs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based fluorescent probe for early diagnosis of pheochromocytoma through identification of circulating tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pheochromocytoma (PCC), as a rare neuroendocrine tumor, is often missed or misdiagnosed because of its atypical clinical manifestations. To realize the early accurate diagnosis of PCC, we have selected circulating tumor cells (CTCs) with more complete biological information as biomarkers and developed a simple and novel fluorescence cytosensor. Octreotide-2,2′,2′′,2′’’- (1,4,7,10 -tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetrayl) tetraacetic acid (DOTA) modified magnetic Fe3O4 and signal amplification CDs@SiO2 nanospheres are prepared to capture and detect PCC-CTCs from peripheral blood via binding to the somatostatin receptor SSTR2 overexpressed on the surface of PCC cells. During the detection process, the target cells were separated and enriched by magnetic capture probes (Fe3O4-DOTA), and then signal probes (CDs@SiO2-DOTA) could also specifically bound to target cells to form the sandwich-like structure for fluorescence signal output. The proposed fluorescence cytosensor has revealed good sensitivity and selectivity for quantitative analysis of PCC-CTCs in the concentration of 5–1000 cells mL−1 with a LOD of 2 cells mL−1. More importantly, designed fluorescence cytosensor has shown good reliability and stability in complex serum samples. This strategy provides a new way for detection of PCC-CTCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pheochromocytoma (PCC), originating in the adrenal medulla or extra-adrenal paraganglia, is a rare neuroendocrine tumor characterized by excessive catecholamine secretion [1,2]. Due to the variable clinical symptoms and extremely low incidence of pheochromocytoma, real-time and accurate diagnosis of pheochromocytoma faces great challenges [3,4]. In response to this problem, researchers have increasingly preferred to choose circulating tumor cells (CTCs) as the ideal biomarkers owing to the more complete biological information and simple detection process [5,6]. Currently, many techniques, including electrochemistry [7,8], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [9,10], fluorescence sensing [11,12], and flow cytometry [13,14], have been developed for efficient capture and highly sensitive detection of CTCs. Among them, fluorescence technique has been widely used in the biomedical field because of its intrinsic advantages of high sensitivity, simplicity, rapidity, and reliability [15]. Recently, various potential fluorescent biosensors for detection of CTCs have been established [16,17,18]. Unfortunately, these biosensors only focus on capture or assay, which is not conducive to integrated functional detection of CTCs [17]. Therefore, the development of a bifunctional integrated sensor with capture and quantification is still urgently needed.

However, looking for suitable targets is faced with great challenges due to the rarity of pheochromocytoma circulating tumor cells (PCC-CTCs) and the lack of specificity of PCC biochemical indicators. The octreotide-2,2′,2′′,2′′′-(1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetrayl) tetraacetic acid (DOTA), a radionuclide-labeled somatostatin (SS) analogue, has been shown to be highly sensitive and accurate in locating metastatic pheochromocytoma and paraganglioma by binding to somatostatin receptor (SSTR) on the tumor surface of PCC cells. Moreover, our previous work has demonstrated that DOTA can be applied to capture and detect rare PCC-CTCs in whole blood [19]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as classical carbon nanomaterials, are generally considered as a promising candidate for biosensing, bioimaging, and other biologically relevant applications due to their low cost, easy and rapid synthesis, great aqueous solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [20,21]. Therefore, developing a novel fluorescence sensor with DOTA as the identification target and CDs as the signal source is of great value for the early diagnosis of PCC through PCC-CTCs monitoring.

In this study, a highly sensitive and specific fluorescence cytosensor was constructed to capture and monitor PCC-CTCs in peripheral blood. As a model cell, PC12 was utilized to characterize PCC-CTCs. During the analysis process, PC12 cells could be captured and enriched through DOTA-modified magnetic nanospheres (Fe3O4-DOTA). Meanwhile, the DOTA-functionalized and CDs-modified SiO2 (CDs@SiO2-DOTA) nanospheres were used as amplified fluorescence signal probes for cell biorecognition signal transduction and output. During the experiment, through the specific recognition of DOTA and STRR2, the capture probes and signaling probes could simultaneously combined on the PC12 cells surface to form a sandwich-like structure (Fe3O4-DOTA/PC12 cells/CDs@SiO2-DOTA), realizing the one-step capture and detection of target cells (Scheme 1 ). This fluorescence cytosensor with integrated capture and quantification can achieve highly sensitive and specific quantitative analysis of PCC-CTCs in whole blood, which provides a new idea for early diagnosis of PCC.

The aminated-Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized based on the previously reported literature [22]. First, FeCl3·6H2O (0.5 g), NaAc (1.0 g), 1,6-hexanediamine (3.25 g), and glycol (15 mL) were mixed and stirred at 50℃. Subsequently, the above mixture was transferred into a high-pressure reactor and heated at 200℃ for 6 h. Finally, the products were rinsed three times using water and ethanol through magnetic separation and dried at 60℃.

The CDs were synthesized via a classical means based on reported literature [23]. Specifically, urea (0.1 g) and citric acid (0.21 g) were dissolved in deionized water (25 mL). After that, the above liquid was poured into a Teflon and heated at 180℃ for 4 h. After natural cooling, the pH of product was regulated to 7.0 by employing NaOH. Finally, the product is diluted with deionized water and placed in refrigerator.

CDs@SiO2 composites were prepared by hydrolysis of TEOS. Briefly, CDs (2.0 mL) and ammonia (200 µL, 25 %) and APTES (100 µL) were introduced into water/ ethanol (50 mL, 2:1, V/V) and magnetic stirred for 30 min. Then, 10 % TEOS ethanol solution (1.0 mL) was dropped to mixed liquid and stirred for 12 h. Finally, the material was acquired through centrifugation and rinsed three times using water and ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles/CDs@SiO2 to DOTA antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@SiO2 was dispersed into 5 mL PBS containing EDC (10 mg) and NHS (5 mg) for 30 min. After removal of remanent EDC and NHS, DOTA (100 µg) was added to incubated with CDs@SiO2 in PBS (5 mL) at 4℃ overnight. Finally, unreacted carboxyl groups were blocked with PBS containing 0.1 wt% Tween 20 and dispersed into 2.5 mL PBS. The process of DOTA modified to Fe3O4 was consistent with the above.

To capture and detect the cells, 160 μL of CDs@SiO2-DOTA signal probes and 40 μL of Fe3O4-DOTA capture probes were simultaneously added into 0.5 mL of PC12 cell suspension solution and incubated at 37 °C (5 % CO2) for 30 min. The cell/probe composites were separated by a magnet, washed, and redispersed, then the fluorescence test was performed.

The CDs were prepared through classical hydrothermal reaction and relevant transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was displayed in Fig. 1 a. The CDs possess punctate nanoparticles morphology with a size of about 2 nm. Similar to most reported carbon dots, the CDs have an obvious ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption peak at around 336 nm (Fig. 1b), which is contributed to n → π* transition of the C = O structure [24]. Additionally, this CDs solution shows strong blue fluorescence under UV light, and the optimum excitation and emission locate at 365 and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). As demonstrated by Fig.S1, CDs reveal similar excitation-dependence, the emission fluorescence (FL) intensity is gradual attenuation as excitation light deviates from 365 nm. Besides, the surface states of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig.S2, the peaks at 3406 and 3153 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H, and the stretching vibration of C = O and C–N appear at 1593 and 1404 cm−1, respectively [25]. Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1c) reveals the existence of C, N, and O elements in the CDs. In the high-resolution spectrum, the C 1 s spectrum can be down into four binding energies at 283.8, 285.28, 287.04, and 287.65 eV, correspond to C–C, C-N, C-O, and C-N/C = O, respectively (Fig. 1d). The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1e) indicates two nitrogen states of C-N-C (398.83 eV) and N–H (400.28 eV). Furthermore, the spectra of O 1 s (Fig. 1f) contain two binding energy states of C = O (530.4 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.0 eV) [26]. The above results indicate that the CDs have been successfully prepared.

The CDs@SiO2 was prepared through solgel method and characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) means. Obviously, CDs@SiO2 exhibits the spherical shape with sizes of several hundred nanometers (Fig. 2 a). Besides, the presence of C, N, O, and Si in CDs@SiO2 are confirmed by XPS technology (Fig. 2b), and the spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Si 2p are described in Fig.S3. Above all, the XPS spectra of Si 2p contain two binding energy states, the energy at 101.48 eV comes from the Si-C bond, and the peak of Si-O bonds is located at 102.18 eV, indicating the successful coating of silica [27]. To prove the aminated Fe3O4 and CDs@SiO2 were conjugated with DOTA by cross-linking reaction to form Fe3O4-DOTA (capture probe) and CDs@SiO2-DOTA (signal probe), the hydrodynamic sizes and zeta potentials of them were measured. Note that the hydrodynamic sizes of both Fe3O4-DOTA and CDs@SiO2-DOTA were increased after the antibody conjugation (Fig.S4), indicating the successful antibody conjugation on the particle surface. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of them reveal the increase of negative charge from −10.1 to −35.6 mV and from 26 to −5 mV (Fig. 2c), respectively, which further demonstrate the success of antibody conjugation. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2d) display enhanced peaks at about 3450 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 are attributed to -N–H and C = O respectively, indicating the existence of covalent amide bonds [17]. This FT-IR result indirectly demonstrates that the DOTA is coupled to the surface of nanoparticle. The fluorescence properties of the CDs@SiO2-DOTA complex were further explored, as shown in Fig. 2e, the optimal excitation of CDs@SiO2-DOTA is about 335 nm while the optimal emission is 433 nm. However, the fluorescence intensity of signal probe gradually diminishes as the excitation wavelength away from 335 nm (Fig. 2f). The results demonstrates that the emitted FL intensity of CDs@SiO2-DOTA is highly relied on the used excitation signal.

Then, the microscopic imaging was performed to verity the targeting ability of the probes. The Fe3O4 and Fe3O4-DOTA were incubated with PC12 cells respectively, and then separated by magnetic separation. As displayed in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, Fe3O4-DOTA + PC12 cells system reveal obvious blue fluorescence, while Fe3O4 + PC12 cells system does not. This phenomenon indicates that Fe3O4-DOTA can specifically capture PC12 cells. In addition, the fluorescence of DAPI and CDs@SiO2-DOTA could be observed after PC12 cells are incubated with Fe3O4-DOTA and CDs@SiO2-DOTA, testifying that CDs@SiO2-DOTA can also specifically recognize PC12 cells (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, the mean fluorescence intensity of PC12 cells was clearly higher after incubated with Fe3O4-DOTA and CDs@SiO2-DOTA. The above results indicate that the designed probes have favorable targeting ability, which could be used for capture and analysis of PCC-CTCs.

To demonstrate the feasibility of proposed fluorescence biosensor for PC12 cells, correlative fluorescence tests were carried out. As testified by Fig. 4 a, no evident fluorescent signal can be observed in Fe3O4-DOTA or Fe3O4-DOTA + CDs@SiO2-DOTA systems. Instead, the Fe3O4-DOTA + CDs@SiO2-DOTA + PC12 cells reveals a distinct fluorescent peak. The above results fully proved the feasibility of fluorescence cytosensor for PC12 cells detection. In addition, the cytotoxicity of the probes was analyzed by MTT method, demonstrating the good biocompatibility of prepared probes towards PC12 cells (Fig.S5).

To achieve the highly sensitive response to PCC-CTCs, several influencing factors including the ratio of signal probe (VSP) to capture probe (VCP), incubation time, and pH value were investigated. The ratio of VSP to VCP is a vital parameter for cell capture capability and signal output performance. As indicated in Fig. 4b, fluorescence intensity increases significantly when the ratio is from 1:3 to 4:1, and then decreases gradually as the ratio continues to increase. Therefore, the optimal ratio of VSP to VCP is 4:1. Besides, the fluorescence signal of this system reaches the maximum value when the buffer pH is 7.4 (Fig. 4c), which may be attributed to the stability of CDs under neutral conditions [28]. As testified by Fig. 4d, the FL intensity of this system arrives at the highest value when reaction time is set as 30 min. Accordingly, the ratio of 4:1 of VSP to VCP, buffer pH = 7.4, and reaction time of 30 min were selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of PC12 cells by cytosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative analysis of PC12 cells via this cytosensor under optimum detection conditions was conducted. As demonstrated by Fig. 5 a, the FL intensity at 433 nm increases with the increasing PC12 cells content in the concentration of 5–103 cells mL−1. The linear fitting diagram of the logarithm of the PC12 cells concentration and fluorescence signal at 433 nm is displayed in Fig. 5b, and the linear fitting equation can be recorded as F433 = 167.03LogCPC12 + 8.971 (R2 = 0.990) with a LOD of 2 cells mL−1. The analytical performance of proposed fluorescence cytosensor for CTCs detection is comparable or better than other assays reported (Table S1). The good linearity and low detection limits indicate the evident advantages of our fluorescence cytosensor for CTCs analysis.

To verify the selectivity in PC12 cells detection of the proposed fluorescence biosensor, a variety of cells including human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) were studied. As demonstrated by Fig. 5c, the signal variations generated from MCF-7 and HepG2 cells are negligible compared with the fluorescence signal corresponding to the PC12 cells. Then, the above cells are mixed to explore the specificity of proposed fluorescence biosensor for PC12 cells analysis. As expected, the similar fluorescence intensity can be observed from the mixture compared with PC12 cells alone system, which can be attributed to the high specificity of the recognition probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of PC12 cells in whole blood
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential value of the proposed fluorescent biosensor in practical applications, the peripheral blood samples from healthy people spiked with different contents of PC12 cells were detected. As demonstrated by Fig. 5d, the fluorescent signal increases gradually with the level of PC12 cells increasing from 10 to 500 cells mL−1 in the peripheral blood, which suggests the good reliability and stability of fluorescence biosensor for PC12 cells even in complex matrix. The fluorescence signal in whole blood is only slightly lower than that in PBS, thus low-level PC12 cells analysis can still be achieved. These outcomes demonstrate that proposed fluorescence method can achieve sensitive and precise assays of PC12 cells in complex matrix, further indicating its application prospect in PCC early diagnosis.

In summary, a fluorescence cytosensor have successfully prepared for PCC-CTCs detection in peripheral blood. Fe3O4-DOTA as capture probes and CDs@SiO2-DOTA as signal probes are used to specifically capture and detect PCC-CTC in one step. The developed fluorescent cytosensor not only exhibits high specificity and selectivity for other non-target cells, but also a better anti-interference ability to blood cells in the peripheral blood. More importantly, this strategy possesses the characteristics of simple operation, low price, and rapid response, which endows it with great potential in clinical disease diagnosis.

Liping Zhao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yiwei Fang: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration. Xinhe Chen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Software. Yang Meng: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology. Fei Wang: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology. Caolong Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC0312602) as well as Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301YD0018C).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123921.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Actuators, B 331 (2021) 129399.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, X. Zhang, J. Tang, Y. Meng, L. Zhao, W. Shi, F. Tao, Z. Kan, F. Wang, C. Li, Dual-Targeting Nanoprobe for Early Diagnosis of Pheochromocytoma Though Coinstantaneous Identification of Circulating Tumor Cells, Anal. Chem. 93(26) (2021) 9036-9040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in carbon quantum dots: properties, fabrication techniques, and bio-applications
